title: CoMMET: To What Extent Can LLMs Perform Theory of Mind Tasks?

sections.title: Abstract
sections.text: Theory of Mind (ToM)-the ability to reason about the mental states of oneself and others-is a cornerstone of human social intelligence. As Large Language Models (LLMs) become ubiquitous in real-world applications, validating their capacity for this level of social reasoning is essential for effective and natural interactions. However, existing benchmarks for assessing ToM in LLMs are limited; most rely solely on text inputs and focus narrowly on belief-related tasks. In this paper, we propose a new multimodal benchmark dataset, CoMMET, a Comprehensive Mental states and Moral Evaluation Task inspired by the Theory of Mind Booklet Task. CoMMET expands the scope of evaluation by covering a broader range of mental states and introducing multiturn testing. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first multimodal dataset to evaluate ToM in a multi-turn conversational setting. Through a comprehensive assessment of LLMs across different families and sizes, we analyze the strengths and limitations of current models and identify directions for future improvement. Our work offers a deeper understanding of the social cognitive capabilities of modern LLMs.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.text:
Theory of Mind (ToM) refers to the ability to attribute mental states to oneself and others (Premack and Woodruff, 1978; Baron-Cohen et al., 1985) . As Large Language Models (LLMs) have been widely adopted in daily life, validating whether LLMs possess ToM capabilities, and to what extent, has become a popular research topic. Understanding how effectively LLMs can interpret human mental states is essential for evaluating their reliability in applications aimed at improving human well-being.

Theory of mind is a complex topic that encompasses multiple mental states (Beaudoin et al., 2020) . Numerous benchmarks have been proposed in recent years (Wu et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2024; Chen et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2025; Villa-Cueva et al., 2025) to better understand the ToM capabilities of LLMs. However, most existing benchmarks continue to focus primarily on belief-related reasoning, rely on text-only inputs (Chen et al., 2025) , and adopt a single-round question-answering format. To address these limitations, we introduce a multimodal benchmark based on ToM booklet tasks (Richardson et al., 2018; Sotomayor-Enriquez et al., 2024) , which covers a broader range of mental states and supports multi-round, story-based question answering.

ToM booklet tasks are established psychological assessments that cover a wide range of ToM concepts with varying levels of difficulty, from reasoning about desires and beliefs to reasoning about moral blameworthiness and mistaken referents. They also include items that assess the understanding of sarcasm and second-order belief-desire reasoning (Figure 1 ). Building on this framework, our benchmark evaluates a broader spectrum of mental states and supports multi-turn evaluation within a story-based conversational setting.

To gain a deeper understanding of LLM performance on this task, we evaluate eight LLMs from four model families, including proprietary models (OpenAI, Gemini, and Claude) as well as open-source models from the LLaMA family. The models are tested on both the original ToM booklet task dataset and our newly constructed dataset derived from it. We provide an in-depth analysis of model performance, highlighting their strengths and limitations, which helps identify directions for future improvement. Overall, our contributions are summarized as follows:

• To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to introduce StoryTurn, an approach that formulates test datasets in a multi-turn format to evaluate LLMs under more realistic, realworld scenarios. The story is presented across multiple rounds, with questions posed at different stages as the narrative unfolds. Images are provided for each round to enhance illustration and contextual understanding. In addition to the story content, we supply adaptive hints for LLMs. For example, if the model answers the first question correctly, we add the feedback: "That's right-Mom thinks Leo still wants the red race car." If the answer is incorrect, we provide a corrective hint: "Remember, Mom didn't hear Leo say that he changed his mind, so she still believes he wants the first toy he picked."

• Our proposed benchmark, CoMMET, is a multimodal dataset that covers a broad range of mental states, including desire, belief, emotion, knowledge, intention, perception, and non-literal communication, as defined in ATOMS (Beaudoin et al., 2020) . It also incorporates moral reasoning grounded in true and false beliefs.

• Our analysis reveals limitations of current LLMs across different types of mental-state reasoning, providing insights for future research.

2 Related Work

Theory of mind benchmarks have been broadly categorized into two types-story-based benchmarks and interactive benchmarks (Chen et al., 2025) . As this paper primarily focuses on story-based benchmarks (Ma et al., 2023b) , we limit our review to benchmarks in this category.

In recent years, numerous story-based benchmarks have been proposed to evaluate the theory of mind capabilities of LLMs. Most existing benchmarks rely on text-only stories (Chen et al., 2024) , including narrative-style stories (Le et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2023) and dialogue-based stories (Kim et al., 2023; Chan et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2025) . These benchmarks typically focus on a narrow range of mental states, mostly focus on beliefrelated reasoning (Wu et al., 2023) or reasoning about white lies (Liu et al., 2025) . A few multimodal benchmarks have also been proposed, such as MMToM-QA (Jin et al., 2024) and MuMA-ToM (Shi et al., 2025) ; however, they mainly assess be-Figure 2 : A scenario analogous to a reference (hard) task from the ToM booklet task. The child asks for the big apple; however, because the leaves block his view, Grandpa cannot see the largest apple at the top of the tree. As a result, he believes the medium apple is the biggest and therefore picks it for the child.

liefs and intentions and are largely restricted to household settings. MOMENTS (Villa-Cueva et al., 2025) is a multimodal video question-answering benchmark that covers all seven mental states defined in the ATOMS taxonomy. Nevertheless, it uses a multiple-choice QA format and evaluates LLMs in a single-turn setting without interaction.

Overall, existing benchmarks primarily rely on text inputs, target only a limited set of mental states, and evaluate models in single-turn, non-interactive settings. To address these limitations, we propose CoMMET, a multimodal benchmark that assesses LLMs' reasoning across diverse mental states in a multi-turn, interactive setting.

sections.title: CoMMET: Introduction and Design
sections.text: Building upon ToM booklet tasks (Richardson et al., 2018; Sotomayor-Enriquez et al., 2024) , we develop a multimodal dataset to further evaluate the theory of mind capabilities of LLMs. In this section, we first provide a brief overview of ToM booklet tasks and then describe how our benchmark is constructed based on this established psychological framework.

sections.title: ToM Booklet Task
sections.text:
Unlike the widely used Sally-Anne test (Baron-Cohen et al., 1985) and Smarties task (Perner et al., 1987) , which primarily focus on belief reasoning, ToM booklet tasks are more challenging and assess a broader range of mental states. Although the ToM booklet task categorizes its test items differently from the ATOMS taxonomy (Beaudoin et al., 2020) , they in fact cover all seven mental states defined by ATOMS. The booklet task includes both relatively simple items that test a single mental state and more complex items that require reasoning over multiple mental states simultaneously.

Figure 2 illustrates a scenario similar to a reference (hard) task from the ToM booklet. In this scenario, there are three apples on a tree (small, medium, and big). However, Grandpa can see only two apples (small and medium); from his perspective, the medium apple is the largest. When the child asks for the big apple, Grandpa will pick the medium apple because he believes it is the biggest one available. This task begins with reasoning about Grandpa's perceptual access (percepts), but answering the question "Which apple will Grandpa pick?" requires an additional step: modeling Grandpa's internal mental representation and understanding that, from his belief, big refers to the medium apple.

In addition to assessing mental state reasoning, the task features advanced components like moral reasoning based on true and false beliefs (e.g., intent to harm). Furthermore, its multi-turn, conversational format (Figure 1 ) makes it more reflective of real-world scenarios. As LLMs continue to evolve, we need more challenging benchmarks to strictly test the extent of their ToM reasoning. Given these strengths, the ToM booklet task provides a solid foundation for building a dataset that both measures and facilitates the further development of these capabilities.

sections.title: CoMMET
sections.text: Although the ToM booklet task is well suited for evaluating LLMs, it is limited in scale. To enable a more systematic assessment of LLMs' theory of mind capabilities, we construct a dataset, CoM-MET, based on the ToM booklet tasks.

sections.title: Multi-Turn Testing Format
sections.text:
While it is natural to assess children using a conversational format, evaluating LLMs requires a structured representation of stories, feedback conditioned on the correctness of previous answers, current questions, images, and responses within a multi-turn framework. To facilitate multi-turn evaluation, we define a StoryTurn format that consists of multiple turns, with each turn specifying the following fields.

• Story: The narrative text for this turn.

• Feedback: The feedback logic (e.g., "If right:

...; If wrong: ..."). Return empty string if not applicable.

• Question: The question to ask LLMs.

• Image: A unique Id for the illustration (e.g., 'img01'). Return empty string if no image.

• Answer: The expected answer. Use semicolons ';' to separate branching answers if needed.

Below is an example of a StoryTurn that evaluates reasoning about "diverse desires". It consists of two turns, with the fields in each turn separated by "|". In this test, both answer sequences-(Apple, Chocolate) and (Chocolate, Apple)-are considered acceptable.

• "Meet Sam.

It is snack time! There are two snacks on the table. There is a shinny red apple, and there is a yummy chocolate bar. | | which snack do you like best, the apple or the chocolate? | 1.png | Apple; Chocolate", • "You do? That is great! But look, Sam likes the other snack better. | |So which snack will Sam pick? The apple or the chocolate? | | Chocolate; Apple"

sections.title: Dataset Construction
sections.text:
To ensure both the diversity and accuracy of the dataset, we leverage a LLM to generate an initial dataset using one-shot learning. In this process, a single example StoryTurn from the ToM booklet task is provided to the LLM as a sample for generating new StoryTurns. After generation, we manually review all generated StoryTurn texts and images to validate their quality and correctness.

Dataset Generation As mentioned previously, rather than treating each question as a separate test item, we introduce StoryTurn to group related questions and evaluate LLMs in a story-based conversational setting. A total of 39 StoryTurns were constructed based on the original ToM booklet task. foot_0 Among them, 31 StoryTurns contain all the information required to answer the questions using text alone, whereas the remaining 8 require visual information in the form of images. Through initial testing of the ToM booklet tasks on state-of-the-art LLMs, we found that Gemini 3.0 Pro foot_1 achieved the best overall performance on both image-dependent and text-only StoryTurns, correctly answering 5 out of 8 image-based Story-Turns and 29 out of 31 text-based ones. In addition, Gemini 3.0 Pro Image 3 , which is specifically designed to handle challenging image generation tasks, produces satisfactory images in most cases, as illustrated in Figure 1 and Figure 2 . Therefore, our pipeline first generates StoryTurns for each task in the original ToM booklet dataset using one-shot prompting with Gemini 3.0 Pro, and then produces corresponding illustrative images for each Story-Turn based on selected segments of the story.

sections.title: Dataset Validation
sections.text:
We manually reviewed all generated StoryTurn texts and found that only one StoryTurn was incorrectly categorized, as it belonged to the sarcasm task rather than the nonsarcasm task. The primary issue in text generation is that the model occasionally produces feedback within the same turn as the answer, instead of deferring it to the subsequent turn. Despite this minor issue, the overall performance in text generation-both in terms of content quality and adherence to the required format-is nearly perfect.

For image generation, we identified several major issues, summarized as follows:

• Incorrect spatial relationships: The model often produces incorrect location information. For example, although the text specifies that the ball is behind the child, the generated image depicts it in an incorrect position.

• Object and character duplication: The same child may appear multiple times in a single image, or be depicted with extra limbs (e.g., three arms). For instance, when a child moves to a new location, the image may still show the child in the previous location. Similar duplication issues also occur for objects.

• Unintended text artifacts: Random or meaningless words sometimes appear in the generated images, even when the prompt explicitly specifies that no text should be included.

• Narrative inconsistencies: Some images conflict with the story context. For example, a mother apologizes for not buying the correct toy, yet the toy is clearly visible and uncov- ered in the image, allowing the child to see it, which contradicts the narrative.

• Unnecessary clues: In some cases, the images either explicitly reveal the correct answer or contain misleading elements that may lead to incorrect responses.

We manually corrected issues in the StoryTurn texts and made multiple attempts to resolve imagerelated problems, ultimately selecting the best available versions. StoryTurns associated with completely incorrect images were removed.

sections.title: Dataset Statistics
sections.text:
After filtering process, we obtained 591 StoryTurns, comprising 1,973 questions and 826 images, to evaluate LLMs across a diverse range of mental states. The average StoryTurn in CoMMET comprises 3.34 conversational turns with questions, with some narratives extending to as many as eight turns. Notably, most StoryTurns probe multiple mental states. For clarity of analysis, each Sto-ryTurn is categorized according to its dominant mental state. As illustrated in Figure 3 , CoMMET spans a broad spectrum of tasks, ranging from simple to complex, and covers all mental states defined in ATOMS, in addition to moral reasoning tasks grounded in false beliefs or intent to cause harm.

Furthermore, as shown in Figure 3 and Table 1: Comparison with two recently published benchmarks that cover nearly all mental states defined in ATOMS. Unlike these benchmarks, we adopt an open-ended format, which is more challenging because no answer options are provided. Additionally, our benchmark includes explicit moral reasoning tasks and evaluates LLMs in conversational settings.

desires), includes explicit "why" questions, and features StoryTurns that simultaneously evaluate multiple mental states, such as second-order belief-desire reasoning. Together, these characteristics enable a more precise assessment of whether LLMs genuinely comprehend the underlying tasks.

sections.title: Experiments
sections.text:
In this section, we describe the experimental setup and evaluation methodology, including the large language models evaluated and the dataset used.

We then provide a detailed analysis of the experimental results, examining model performance across different task categories, mental states, and levels of complexity, and discuss the broader implications of these findings.

Models Image Overall Belief Emotion Intention Desire Percept NLC Belief_Emotion Belief_Desire NLCE MBK LLaMA-3.2-11B No 47.08% 55.24% 80.95% 19.05% 100.00% 57.14% 58.14% 71.43% 23.81% 45.24% 5.95% Yes 46.00% 63.81% 85.71% 19.05% 97.62% 38.10% 46.51% 57.14% 23.81% 47.62% 7.14% LLaMA-4-Maverick No 55.94% 74.29% 85.71% 14.29% 100.00% 61.90% 48.84% 90.48% 28.57% 76.19% 5.95% Yes 55.51% 69.52% 85.71% 19.05% 100.00% 47.62% 55.81% 90.48% 23.81% 80.95% 5.95% Claude-Sonnet-3.5 No 71.71% 81.90% 100.00% 40.48% 100.00% 76.19% 67.44% 88.10% 38.10% 97.62% 41.67% Yes 74.08% 82.86% 100.00% 42.86% 100.00% 76.19% 72.09% 97.62% 42.86% 97.62% 44.05% Claude-Sonnet-4.5 No 83.59% 94.29% 100.00% 64.29% 100.00% 85.71% 67.44% 95.24% 33.33% 95.24% 76.19% Yes 84.02% 92.38% 100.00% 59.52% 100.00% 76.19% 67.44% 95.24% 61.90% 92.86% 79.76% GPT-4o No 77.75% 92.38% 100.00% 64.29% 100.00% 80.95% 65.12% 90.48% 28.57% 92.86% 53.57% Yes 79.70% 92.38% 100.00% 54.76% 100.00% 76.19% 76.74% 92.86% 38.10% 97.62% 58.33% GPT-5.2-Chat No 89.20% 98.10% 100.00% 69.05% 100.00% 90.48% 69.77% 97.62% 47.62% 92.86% 94.05% Yes 88.12% 94.29% 100.00% 73.81% 100.00% 85.71% 69.77% 92.86 71.43% 92.86% 88.10% Gemini-3.0-Flash No 87.04% 97.14% 100.00% 71.43% 100.00% 90.48% 69.77% 100.00% 33.33% 92.86% 84.52% Yes 87.26% 93.33% 100.00% 78.57% 100.00% 90.48% 95.35% 97.62% 14.29% 83.33% 84.52% Gemini-3.0-Pro No 92.22% 97.14% 100.00% 95.24% 97.62% 95.24% 76.74% 100.00% 66.67% 92.86% 89.29% Yes 92.22% 92.38% 100.00% 95.24% 100.00% 90.48% 95.35% 97.62% 66.67% 90.48% 88.10% Additionally, we test the LLaMA 3.2 11B Vision-Instruct foot_2 model to assess how smaller models perform on these tasks.

Dataset As mentioned previously, CoMMET contains a total of 591 StoryTurns. Among these, 463 StoryTurns provide all the necessary information in text form, while the remaining 128 require complementary information from images to answer specific questions. Consequently, we conduct experiments and perform analyses separately on these two subsets of StoryTurns.

Evaluation In this paper, we report story accuracy (Le et al., 2019) at multiple levels of granularity, including overall performance across all tasks and accuracy within each mental state category. Story accuracy is defined such that a story is considered correctly answered only if all questions associated with that story are answered correctly.

Given that our dataset consists of open-ended question-answering tasks and involves large-scale evaluations across diverse task types and LLMs, we adopt LLM-based judges for the initial evaluation. Specifically, we use Gemini 3.0 Flash foot_3 to compare model outputs with the ground-truth answers and determine their correctness. To mitigate potential judgment errors introduced by the LLM evaluator and to enhance reliability, we manually validate the evaluation results.

sections.title: Main Results
sections.text:
Tables 2 and 3 present the performance of different LLMs on two CoMMET subsets: the subset in which all information required to answer the questions is contained in the text, and the subset in which visual information is required. From the results, we draw the following observations:

• Achieving high story-level accuracy remains challenging, as few LLMs attain 90% storylevel accuracy on either subset.

• CoMMET tasks are particularly difficult for smaller models (e.g., LLaMA-3.2-11B-Vision-Instruct), indicating that further improvements are needed to enhance their performance on this type of reasoning task.

• When all necessary information is already present in the text, adding images for illustration does not yield consistent performance improvements. However, in scenarios where the question depends on visual attributes (e.g., shapes), visual input can still enhance performance even when the textual information is complete, as illustrated by the NLC mental state in Table 2 .

• Most LLMs perform well on simpler emotion and desire tasks, achieving near-perfect accuracy. However, further improvements are needed for more complex tasks involving multiple mental states.

sections.title: LLMs Perform Well on Some Tasks
sections.text: As shown in Tables 2 and 3 , LLMs perform well on a subset of tasks, particularly those involving relatively simple mental state reasoning.

sections.title: Mental State: Emotion
sections.text: This task typically involves a two-turn StoryTurn in which a child's past experiences or memories are described. LLMs are then asked how the child would feel if they encountered the same scenario again, and what they would do in response. This task is relatively easy for LLMs, and most models achieve strong performance.

sections.title: Mental State: Desire
sections.text: This task also uses a two-turn StoryTurn to evaluate common and diverse desires. First, LLMs are asked to select one object from a pair that a child prefers. Next, they are told the preference of another child-either the same object or the other one-and asked which object the second child would choose. Almost all LLMs perform perfectly on this task, including the smaller models.

sections.title: Moral Reasoning
sections.text:
As mentioned previously, we evaluate moral reasoning in CoMMET. Typically, each story is followed by a question such as "Is it mean that the child . . . ? Why?" We include stories in which the child performs the action either intentionally or accidentally. Our results show that most large language models can reason about these scenarios correctly and provide appropriate explanations. However, smaller language models still struggle to give accurate judgments and justifications.

4.4 Do LLMs Truly Understand the Tasks?

While LLMs demonstrate near-perfect performance on relatively simple tasks, their effectiveness declines significantly on more challenging tasks, especially those requiring explanatory ("why") reasoning.

sections.title: Mental States: Belief and Desire
sections.text: Taking the second-order belief-desire task (Figure 1 ) as an example, we observe that after validation, most errors occur in the final turn, when the model is asked why a character is surprised or confused. The most common error is answering that he believed his mother did not know he had changed his mind. Notably, even Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 3.0 Flash, Gemini 3.0 Pro and GPT 5.2 Chat exhibit this error pattern, calling into question whether LLMs genuinely possess ToM capabilities.

sections.title: Mental States: Intention
sections.text:
We take the hard reference task-an imagedependent task from the ToM Booklet-as an example. In this scenario, illustrated in Figure 4 , Marie's mother leaves three cookies of different sizes in the kitchen: a small cookie on the counter, a medium-sized cookie on the table, and a large cookie on a shelf. Marie and her father then enter so that her father can pack her a snack for school. Marie tells her father, "I want the big cookie." The question is: "Which cookie will the father pack for Marie?" Most models select the large cookie, including Gemini 3.0 Pro and GPT-5.2-Chat. When we subsequently provide the information "Actually, the father packs the medium cookie" and ask "Why does the father pack this cookie?", these models respond with explanations Consequently, he believes the medium cookie is the largest available and therefore packs it for the child.

such as "The big one may be too large for a snack, so the medium one is more appropriate." LLaMA-4-Maverick produces the correct answer to the first question; however, its explanation for the second question-"Because Marie's mom said to take the one in the middle"-is entirely hallucinated, as no such information appears in the story.

sections.title: Mental State: Non-Literal Communication and Emotion
sections.text:
Taking the sarcasm and non-sarcasm tasks as examples, each StoryTurn contains two questions.

The first asks why the child says a particular utterance (e.g., "Mmm, my favorite") in either unhappy or happy scenarios. The second question asks about the child's emotional state. We find that LLMs-including GPT-5.2-Chat and Claude Sonnet 3.5-answer some sarcasm questions incorrectly. Smaller models perform substantially worse; for example, LLaMA-3.2-11B-Vision-Instruct achieves only 4.55% accuracy on the sarcasm task.

sections.title: Mental State: Belief
sections.text:
Unlike the widely studied false-belief task in the Sally-Anne test and Smarties task, the ToM Booklet includes a diverse belief task. In this task, a child searches for an object that may be located in either location A or location B. The first question asks LLMs to predict where the object actually is.

Next, the model is told that the child believes the object is not at that location, and the second question asks where the child will go to look for the object. In the third turn, the child fails to find the object at the location he/she believed it to be, and the model is asked to infer the object's true location. Our evaluation shows that LLMs frequently make errors in this third turn. Models sometimes predict a location other than A or B, or respond that the location is unknown. This pattern suggests that LLMs do not robustly distinguish between reality and belief in this task.

In conclusion, although LLMs demonstrate strong performance on certain relatively simple tasks, we argue that they do not yet exhibit consistent or robust theory of mind capabilities. Their success is often task-specific and does not reliably generalize to more complex scenarios involving diverse beliefs, perspective-taking, or explanatory reasoning. This observation aligns with findings from prior studies (Ullman, 2023; Ma et al., 2023a; Kim et al., 2023; Verma et al., 2024; Zhu et al., 2024; Bortoletto et al., 2024; Shapira et al., 2024; Chen et al., 2024) ., which similarly report that apparent competence in isolated tasks does not necessarily reflect a genuine or stable theory of mind understanding.

sections.title: Conclusion
sections.text: In this paper, we propose CoMMET, a benchmark inspired by the psychological theory of mind booklet tasks. CoMMET is a multimodal dataset that encompasses all the mental states defined in ATOMS and additionally includes moral reasoning. Unlike traditional approaches that present the complete story and ask questions only at the end, CoM-MET evaluates LLMs across multiple conversational turns, providing feedback based on their answers to previous questions. This approach offers a more naturalistic evaluation, akin to how children are tested in real-world scenarios. To enhance interpretability and reduce ambiguity, we map the diverse tasks in CoMMET to their corresponding ATOMS mental states. We evaluate a broad range of LLMs from multiple model families, including both state-of-the-art and smaller models, to examine how model scale influences performance across these tasks. Through detailed comparisons and analyses, we identify key areas requiring further improvement. We believe that our benchmark dataset, together with the limitations revealed in this study, offers valuable insights and concrete guidance for future research on advancing robust theory of mind reasoning in LLMs.
